questions.text: Which 3 strategies help reduce perioperative risk in obese patients undergoing minimally invasive gynecologic surgery? Select 3 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Trendelenburg angle >30° for optimal visualization
B) Use of optical trocar for abdominal access
C) Closing all fascial ports regardless of size
D) Tucking arms with padding rather than shoulder braces
E) Using Palmer’s point entry in select patients
F) Use of 5000u of preop heparin for DVT prophylaxis

questions.explanation: Optical trocars aid safer access; arms should be tucked and padded (avoid shoulder braces); LUQ/Palmer’s point can be safer with thick abdominal walls. Routine closure of <10 mm ports and universal preop heparin are not indicated.

questions.text: Which of the following intraoperative risks are increased in patients with obesity undergoing laparoscopy? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: B) Aspiration due to increased intra-abdominal pressure
E) Brachial plexus injury from body shifting

questions.explanation: Obesity: ↓pulmonary compliance → ventilation difficulty, ↑aspiration risk, and higher risk of brachial plexus injury from shifting in Trendelenburg. Cardiac output often increases, and oxygen reserve decreases.

questions.text: Which of the following are incorrect regarding surgical care during pregnancy? Select all that are incorrect.

answerOptions.text: A) Laparoscopy is contraindicated in the third trimester
B) Port sites ≥10 mm should be closed
C) Corpus luteum removal before 10 weeks requires progesterone support
D) Supine positioning improves maternal venous return
E) Lower pneumoperitoneum pressures are advised

questions.explanation: Laparoscopy is acceptable in all trimesters; avoid supine positioning (use left lateral tilt). Closing ≥10 mm ports, progesterone if CL removed <10 weeks, and lower intra-abdominal pressures are correct statements.

questions.text: Which changes in pregnancy most directly influence laparoscopic surgical planning? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: E) Increased sensitivity to inhalational anesthetics

questions.explanation: ↑CO and ↓FRC drive intraoperative adjustments (e.g., lower insufflation pressure, lateral tilt). Other listed changes are less directly determinative for port/pressure planning.

questions.text: Which of the following are appropriate surgical considerations in elderly or medically complex patients undergoing laparoscopy? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: A) Limit Trendelenburg due to reduced diaphragmatic reserve
B) Use lower insufflation pressures to preserve renal blood flow
C) Opioids are well tolerated in elderly due to slowed metabolism
D) Monitor for atypical symptom presentation
E) Expect reduced sensitivity to anesthetic agents

questions.explanation: Limit Trendelenburg, consider lower IAP, and anticipate atypical presentations. Elderly are more sensitive to opioids/anesthetics—not less.

questions.text: Which factors most increase the risk of postoperative respiratory depression in older patients undergoing gynecologic laparoscopy? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: B) Age-related changes in neurotransmitter function
C) Reduced ventilatory response to hypercapnia
E) Use of lactated Ringer’s solution intraoperatively

questions.explanation: Aging → altered CNS neurotransmission and blunted hypercapnic/hypoxic drive, increasing risk of hypoventilation with sedatives/opioids.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial trocar entry site for a morbidly obese patient undergoing laparoscopic surgery?
questions.explanation: Left upper quadrant (Palmer’s point) avoids displaced umbilicus and thickened wall, improving safety.

questions.text: Which of the following physiologic changes in obesity contributes most to increased anesthetic risk during laparoscopic surgery?
questions.explanation: Obesity → restrictive physiology (↓FRC, ↓compliance) → higher anesthetic/ventilatory risk.

questions.text: Which positioning technique is most associated with brachial plexus injury in obese patients undergoing laparoscopic surgery?

answerOptions.text: B) Tucking the arms with arm sleds
C) Use of wider boot stirrups

questions.explanation: Shoulder braces can cause traction on the brachial plexus as patients slide in Trendelenburg.

questions.text: Which laparoscopic access site is most appropriate for a gravid uterus at 20 weeks gestation?
questions.explanation: LUQ (Palmer’s point) avoids the enlarged uterus in mid-pregnancy.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step for a 12-week pregnant patient presenting with a 7 cm simple adnexal mass and absent Doppler flow?

answerOptions.text: C) IV fluid resuscitation and observation
D) Urgent laparoscopy for detorsion and possible cystectomy

questions.explanation: Absent flow suggests torsion → urgent laparoscopy for detorsion/cystectomy.

questions.text: Which of the following physiologic changes in pregnancy contributes most to the increased risk of aspiration during surgery?

answerOptions.text: B) Increased intra-abdominal pressure and gastroesophageal reflux

questions.explanation: Gravid uterus → ↑IAP and LES relaxation → reflux/aspiration risk.

questions.text: What age-related physiologic change increases the risk of postoperative respiratory depression?

answerOptions.text: C) Reduced ventilatory response to hypercapnia

questions.explanation: Older adults have a blunted CO₂/O₂ ventilatory drive, heightening risk with sedatives/opioids.

questions.text: What is the most important consideration when determining laparoscopic access in a patient with a history of multiple abdominal surgeries?
questions.explanation: Prior operations → adhesions/distorted landmarks → choose access site accordingly.

questions.text: What intraoperative factor most contributes to renal hypoperfusion in laparoscopic surgery for medically complex patients?
questions.explanation: Elevated IAP from pneumoperitoneum reduces renal perfusion and GFR.
